frameworks.name: confirmation bias
frameworks.one_line: Internal beliefs bias subsequent sensory sampling toward belief-consistent evidence.

frameworks.name: active sensing
frameworks.one_line: Eye-movements treated as belief-driven information-sampling actions.

frameworks.name: covert spatial attention
frameworks.one_line: Attention modeled as belief-conditioned weighting of spatial locations.

frameworks.name: model-based psychophysics
frameworks.one_line: Quantitative model links internal beliefs to fixations and saccades.

core_question: Do confirmation biases observed in explicit decisions also shape eye-movements during active sensing?
key_assumptions: Eye-movements reflect belief-driven sampling rather than purely stimulus-driven capture.
Attention and saccades share an underlying belief representation.
A 2AFC orientation discrimination task can isolate belief influences on sampling.
manipulations: Explicit attentional cue to upper vs. lower hemifield.
Free eye-movements allowed during the discrimination task (active sensing).
Spatially separated elementary stimuli (removing perceptual fusion confounds).
key_findings: Internal beliefs measurably bias covert spatial attention.
Subjects fixated and saccaded preferentially to belief-consistent locations.
Confirmation biases in eye-movements resemble those previously seen in explicit decisions and overt attention.
interpretations: Eye-movements during active sensing inherit the same confirmation bias seen in explicit decisions.
Results support a tight link between attention and saccadic sampling under belief-driven decision-making.
limitations_noted: Previous setup confounded attentional and early-sensory effects due to perceptual fusion — addressed here by spatial separation.
